query: Angular unsubscribe patterns and memory leaks interview question

competitors.label: Angular takeUntilDestroyed Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This is the official Angular guide for the modern teardown helper, so it is the canonical baseline for unsubscribe guidance.
competitors.winReason: The Angular guide is authoritative on takeUntilDestroyed, but our page is broader because it compares async pipe, takeUntilDestroyed, classic takeUntil, manual teardown, and non-RxJS leaks in one answer.
theirStrengths: It is the official reference for takeUntilDestroyed and DestroyRef usage.
theirGaps: It is intentionally focused on one teardown tool rather than the full unsubscribe decision tree.

realWorldUseCases: Most Angular memory leaks are not mysterious framework bugs. They come from long-lived streams, timers, or global listeners that outlive a component.
followUpConfusion: The production question is not “unsubscribe from everything?” but “which sources complete on their own, and which ones keep consuming memory, CPU, or network until I tear them down?”

realWorldUseCases: The guide prevents a common leak pattern, but it is narrower than our broader teardown taxonomy.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev includes a solid takeUntilDestroyed example, so the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: It explains injection-context nuances for takeUntilDestroyed, but our page better answers when unsubscribe boilerplate is unnecessary and when non-RxJS cleanup still matters.

actionableExamples: Add one resolver-focused teardown helper example that combines DestroyRef and route cleanup in the same snippet if we want to beat the official guide on pure examples.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Experienced Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd directly covers unhandled subscriptions, async pipe, takeUntilDestroyed, and RxJS pitfalls in Angular apps.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd surfaces unsubscribe pitfalls, but our page is broader and more decision-complete because it compares auto-completing sources, async pipe, takeUntilDestroyed, classic takeUntil, and non-RxJS leaks.
theirStrengths: It is concise, interview-friendly, and already names manual subscribe leaks and takeUntilDestroyed.
theirGaps: It is more checklist-like and less exhaustive about source lifetimes and non-RxJS cleanup.

realWorldUseCases: Most Angular memory leaks are not mysterious framework bugs. They come from long-lived streams, timers, or global listeners that outlive a component.
actionableExamples: Route-level pitfall: resolver streams that never complete
followUpConfusion: Leaks don’t only come from Observables. addEventListener, setInterval, and 3rd-party libraries must also be cleaned up in ngOnDestroy.

realWorldUseCases: The experienced guide calls out unhandled subscriptions and RxJS pitfalls, but it is less complete on which sources complete automatically and which do not.
actionableExamples: Its takeUntilDestroyed and switchMap snippet keeps the example comparison close.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on the non-RxJS leak surface and on the nuance that some Angular Observables complete on their own.

actionableExamples: Add one compact before-versus-after snippet that keeps HttpClient one-shot and valueChanges leak patterns in the same code block.

competitors.label: Angular University 3 Common RxJs Pitfalls
competitors.selectionReason: This is a strong tutorial/reference competitor because it explains several real RxJS failure modes in Angular, including duplicate requests and resolver completion.
competitors.winReason: Angular University remains strong on RxJS pitfalls, but our page now wins as a teardown guide because it combines component lifetimes, non-RxJS cleanup, explicit DestroyRef usage, and the resolver-completion trap in one answer.
theirStrengths: It is practical and organized around real RxJS pitfalls developers hit in Angular.
theirGaps: It is narrower than our teardown-focused answer because it does not aim to be a full unsubscribe decision guide.

realWorldUseCases: Most Angular memory leaks are not mysterious framework bugs. They come from long-lived streams, timers, or global listeners that outlive a component.
actionableExamples: Route-level pitfall: resolver streams that never complete

realWorldUseCases: The article covers real Angular RxJS failure modes, but our page is broader on leak sources and teardown choices.
actionableExamples: Its pitfall walkthroughs are practical enough that the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: It helps with async-pipe and resolver-related follow-ups, so the deeper confusion comparison remains close.
